sentences.text: Good afternoon, Mrs.
Vos.
And thank you very much for joining us at the Shoah Foundation and telling us your experiences during World War II as a rescuer.
We'd like to start with your name and could you spell it, please.
It's-- the name is Johtje Vos.
And you spell it J-O-H-T-J-E, which is a short name for Johanna, which is my official name.
But nobody [INAUDIBLE] calls me so.
And Vos is the last name, V-O-S.
J-O-H-A-N-N-A.
Yeah.
I had a second name was Hendrika.
But I threw that out.
H-E-N-D-R-I-K-A.
No.
Johanna Hendrika Kuyper.
Kuyper.
K-U-Y-P-E-R.
Yes.
Nono.
N-O-N-O.
[LAUGHTER]
That was because my older sister couldn't say Johtje and she said [DUTCH] And I myself said, no, no.
And [DUTCH] means little nut.
[LAUGHTER]
My birthday is December 29, 1909.
In Amersfoort.
A-M-E-R-S-F-O-O-R-T.
In Holland.
Yeah.
I was born in Amersfoort.
Yeah.
My father was a career officer, so he was moved once in awhile.
No.
The next one was Breda and that I remember.
Breda is in the south of Holland.
[LAUGHS] I remember an accident.
There were horses who were-- had lost control.
Two horses.
And I was playing in the garden and the horses came straight to our garden.
And my mother yelled at me, and said, stand completely still.
Don't move.
Don't move.
I don't know why that was the best thing to do, but anyway.
They didn't hurt me.
But that I-- it's one of my earliest remembrances.
Beg your pardon?
I don't know.
Middle, middle-size place.
It was an-- garrison-- military place.
Just Dutch people.
It was not an--
I don't know.
I was too young.
I don't remember much of it.
I know my father was a-- was the major of the town.
He was an officer.
No, a major.
Yeah.
Yes.
I do remember the house.
It was a typical stone Dutch house with a garden in front and a garden in the back and a little iron fence.
They're all memories I have of it.
Yeah.
That I don't know.
No.
I think it was a rented house.
Because my father was moved from one town to the other as an officer.
In Leiden.
L-E-I-D-E-N.
That's a university town.
[WHISTLES] You ask difficult questions.
[LAUGHS]
[LAUGHS] I'd think six or seven.
Because I was in elementary school in Leiden.
Yes.
Yes.
Yeah.
In the army.
And my father was commander of Leiden, of that city.
Commander means that he was the highest officer there.
And it was in a military town.
I mean, there was a big group of military people, anyway.
Well, he was very involved in politic-- with-- because his father was prime minister of Holland.
And that was, of course, a very political position.
His father.
Abraham Kuyper.
His father's name was Kuyper.
Abraham.
Abraham.
You know.
Prime minister of Holland.
Well--
--during the first world war, my father was at the frontier and-- of the-- of the country.
And my mother and my two sisters and I lived with my grandfather.
So I have a very lively memory of all that.
And it was interesting because, you know, Prince Hendrik would come and visit and, you know, and things like that, so-- I sat on his knees one day.
So it was-- so, yeah.
It was an interesting time for me.
Yeah.
Well, our political party, which was founded by my grandfather, was anti-revolutionary.
A
And that's still an-- party, large party in Holland.
[LAUGHS] I can't.
I don't know.
[LAUGHS] I don't know.
I'm-- I'm totally uninterested in-- in politics.
Oh, yes.
And still.
It's still in power.
It was an-- a Christian party.
Well, religious.
Yes.
Well, there were schools that were Christian schools.
And there was a Christian high school, and a Christian University, which was founded by my grandfather.
And, yeah, we went, of course, to Christian schools, which meant that we had an-- we all came-- came to all our-- all the classes together and they read something from the Bible and we prayed.
And then we went all to our classrooms and we didn't hear about religion anymore.
But that was the-- the spirit of it.
Protestant.
Yeah.
It was called Christian reformed.
Dutch reformed, Yeah.
Yeah.
That's still in Holland.
Oh, sure.
No.
No.
Oh, there were public, public schools.
Oh, sure.
More public schools than Christian schools.
Oh, yes.
Yeah.
We lived in a dead end lane and-- very [INAUDIBLE] lane in Leiden.
And we had a young people's club there.
And there were all the Catholic children and they were all [INAUDIBLE], you know?
So--
Well, I left home when I-- when my parents were still in Leiden.
And I was, I think, 19 years old.
I grew up in Leiden, yeah.
Yeah.
Yes.
Well, there were the intellectual people and the professional people, and the people who did other work.
And Holland was quite conscious about differences in that time.
It's not so now anymore.
There was not a special Jewish community.
There were Jewish people living everywhere.
But the Jewish community, I've never heard of in Holland.
Oh, yeah.
I had a Jewish boyfriend.
In Leiden.
Yeah.
Well, in-- in our religion, it was an unusual thing.
And it was-- it didn't please my parents.
Not because he was Jewish, but he was not a believer in God.
If he had-- my sister married a Jewish man, and-- but he was a believer.
And so my parents found that perfectly all right.
But my boyfriend was an atheist.
And of course, that was not accepted in my family.
I went to Paris.
We-- We just-- he was a doctor.
And when-- I was a student-- medical student.
And when he made his-- was ready with his-- with his study, he went to Indonesia, on purpose, so that we could-- and I went to Paris.
So we both went to completely different atmosphere.
Because he couldn't marry me, because he was an-- an-- an-- how you call that-- he-- he couldn't-- his children couldn't be Jewish when he married to Christian girl.
That was the reason.
And when I lived for a year in Paris, he came and said, you are more dear to me than-- (LAUGHS) than-- than the religion.
And so he make up his mind that he want to marry me.
But he was too late.
(LAUGHS) I see.
I was over it.
[LAUGHTER]
Oh yes, sure.
There was-- well, I don't even know who was Jewish and who was not Jewish.
That-- That was not the point.
You know--
Beg your pardon?
Oh, sure.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I liked-- I liked certain festivities and to celebrate with them.
That-- the day which is around Easter-- what's that name again?
Yeah.
Passover.
No.
As far as I knew, there was-- there was no anti-Semitism in Holland.
As far as I know.
I've never noticed or anything.
Never heard of it.
So the Jewish people in Germany would all flee, when Hitler came, to Holland, of course, because they felt safe there.
And they were safe there.
No.
No, I can't say that.
Well--
--they wanted-- they didn't want to-- me to marry my Jewish boyfriend, not because he was Jewish, but because he was not a believer in God.
And that was the first question my father asked of everybody.
Well, he became a believer.
He converted.
Yes.
So he became a Christian.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Totally accepted, yeah.
But they lived not in the same country.
They lived in-- in London.
My sister was a doctor and she had her practice in London.
Yes.
I think I was 19 years-- 20 about.
I don't remember.
Oh, it was-- it was before the rise of Nazism--
Yes.
Yeah-- no.
Hitler was already there and my first husband-- I'm-- I've been married twice--
--and my first husband left Holland.
And he was an artist.
And he went to Paris, not only because he was an artist, but also because he didn't want to live in Germany with Hitler.
He was not Jewish.
But he was just very anti-Hitler.
No, never lived in Germany.
--you were living in Holland.
We lived in Paris.
In-- and then you moved to Paris.
And then-- no.
We married in Paris and lived in Paris.
And then when we had-- started to have children, we went back to Holland.
Oh, terrible.
Well, we heard all about it.
Because, you know, Holland and Germany are practically one country.
[LAUGHS] And so everybody I knew was incredibly upset and-- and-- and miserable about it.
Even before we were occupied ourselves.
Yes.
Yes.
There was a Nazi party, yeah.
All kind of people.
Yeah, Dutch people.
Yeah.
There was a Dutch Nazi party.
And we had our own Hitler.
We had [NON-ENGLISH] Mussert, who was the head of the-- of the National Socialistic Party.
Mussert.
M-U-S-S-E-R-T.
There was not a Jewish community.
I mean--
The Jewish who lived there, well, they all were scared of Mussert, of course.
No.
But we hid them.
We hid the Jewish people.
No.
That was as soon as Hitler started to enter-- in Holland.
No.
Prior to that, we didn't have to protect anybody, with nothing happened to them yet.
Yeah.
Yeah.
But Mussert started a little after Hitler in-- in-- had started in Germany, of course.
And then when Germany came to-- how you say-- occupied Holland, then Mussert was there.
But he was under the thumb of Hitler.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
In 1939 and earlier, '38, with Kristallnacht--
Yeah.
--when there were all these laws that went into effect in Germany--
Yeah.
--Jews began to migrate to--
To Holland.
Yeah.
Yes.
Well, they found jobs and they were-- people were very nice to them.
They welcomed them.
I mean, they didn't ask them to come, but when they came and had to flee-- there were, of course-- there were also people in Holland who resented it.
But the majority of the Dutch people were very anti-Hitler-- the vast majority-- and so the helped the Jewish people.
Beg your pardon?
Well, I couldn't-- they talked about it, and the wrote in the paper about it, you know, and things like that.
But for-- they didn't attack them, or they didn't do anything to them.
But they were just resentful, they didn't like it that so many Jewish people came to Holland.
But they couldn't do anything about it.
Because in general, the Dutch people agreed fully to help.
Oh, sure.
Yeah, I-- I don't remember that well.
That was in 1940.

Then I was-- yeah, then I was married, and-- yeah, I remember that a lot of-- I-- I remember that first of all, a lot of Jewish people came to Holland, and that they had a terrible shock when the Germans invaded our country.
And they-- most of them-- many of them tried to flee to England.
Certainly, and [LAUGHS] everybody who had a boat, or could get near a boat, went.
Because that was not such a far trip, not even to England.
And the ones who were-- stayed in Holland, started to hide.
So we had a lot of Jewish people in our house.
By that time I was married.
I was married to my second husband.
But I still had-- my first husband was German.
And so in Holland, when a young girl in Holland-- or a woman in Holland-- marries a foreigner, she loses her-- in that time-- her nationality.
So I became a German.
And even when I was divorced, and married my Dutch husband, I still was German.
Because I could not apply, because it was in the war that I married.
And I couldn't apply because I couldn't attract any attention to us, because we had so many Jewish people hidden.
Yeah, German citizen.
Well, all my friends and everybody I knew, my family, knew that I was not an alien, but--
And other people didn't know, I mean, I didn't tell it around, I am German.
Yeah.
Because I had a-- for instance, we had distribution cards.
And I got double distribution cards, because I was German.
And it was wonderful, because I could help the Jewish people with that.
They didn't get the-- they didn't get the distribution cards.
So when I got double ones for myself and my two, then, German daughters, I could use them to help the Jewish people who were hidden in our house.
No.
No.
They didn't know that I was German.
I mean, and I didn't give myself up to any group, or whatever.
I was in Leiden, yeah.
Yeah.
[LAUGHS] I don't remember.
No.
Oh, yeah.
He occupied every--
Every town or village and city was occupied.
Well, they confiscated places-- for instance, many Jewish houses, from Jews who had fled, were taken by the Germans.
And then they had the [NON-ENGLISH], and, you know, and, you know, bridges.
Oh, yes.
We saw them all the time, yeah.
We hated those green uniforms.
[LAUGHS] Walking through our Dutch villages and see these.
Well, it was-- it came in the papers.
We read about it, and, you know-- very shortly after the Germans had-- had occupied Holland.
And there were-- there were German papers, certainly, in Holland, German newspapers.
And we didn't want to read them.
So then they put it also in the Dutch papers.
And so we knew all about, yeah.
There was nothing secret about it.
Beg your pardon?
I don't really remember.
That we all had to register ourselves.
And, of course, we were not allowed to have Jewish people in our house-- visiting, or living, or whatever.
And things like that, you know.
No, not every Jew looks Jewish.
I didn't mean by-- I meant by clothing, or by--
No, oh no.
Yeah, they had to wear the star.
You know.
They had that yellow, big star.
But when that was an order, that we-- that all the Jewish people had to wear that star, we took stars, too, and wore them, to mix the Germans up.
But by that time, we had a lot of Jewish people in our house, already, hidden.
And they begged my husband and me not to wear that star, because they said you'd attract attention to yourself with that.
So we didn't do it.
We took it off again.
No, I have never seen it myself.
But my husband went off into Amsterdam.
We lived in the country.
And when my husband went to Amsterdam, he always came home all shook up, and pale and miserable from what he had seen there.
Well, he had seen Jewish people being thrown in-- in open animal carts from the train, you know, and-- you know, and--
My husband was an agriculturist.
No.
He-- he-- what did he actually do in the war?
He did nothing but trying to find food for his family.
And afterwards, he-- well, after the war, we came to America.
And here, we had a children's home.
Well, for the first time, I had fear.
And-- and we started immediately to be very active to help the Jewish people.
So that-- that included, also, a lot of fear, of course, and danger.
So our life-- our life changed very much.
Well, they were in need of help.
I mean, it's like when you see a child fall in the water, you jump in and get it out.
I wouldn't, because I can't swim.
[LAUGHS] But-- but every other decent people will do it.
And that's the same thing.
We had Jewish friends and.
We heard about all that-- all the laws that came out to the Jews, so we said we'll help them.
So it started out with the child.
The Jewish people in our village, or in the villages, had-- were forced to come to a Jewish part in-- in Amsterdam.
And when those friends of ours, who were very famous musicians, when they were ordered to go to Amsterdam, they had a little boy.
And they were extremely worried to take the child there, because they didn't know what was going to happen.
So we said, well, give him to us.
And we took him.
Yes.
Nap-- N-A-P-- that was his first name-- De-- D-E-- Klyn-- K-L-Y-N.
And he was a very famous violinist.
Yeah.
She was a pianist, and her name was Alice.
A-L-I-S-E.
C-E.
Mark.
And they stayed-- they left the child with you.
They left the child.
And they also were in hiding, and when there was a difficulty, or a danger, then they came to us.
But we lived in the same village where they lived.
And so it was too dangerous to have them with us-- for them.
Because people who would come to us might recognize them.
So they-- were not hidden-- they once in awhile, for a week or so, when there was trouble, they would come to us.
But in general, they were hidden in Amsterdam with other people.
Which people, you mean?
Well, people who came to our house.
And we became very, very careful with who came to our house.
But of course, they had a room upstairs.
And they-- and-- so, sometimes we had people who we didn't know-- want to know that we had Jewish people in the house.
But our friends and all knew it.
And they had did this themselves, the same as we did.
Your children knew that there were Jewish--
My children knew, of course, that they were living with us, but they didn't know they were Jewish.
I mean, we never talked about that.
Oh.
